title: Zero-field dipolar decoupling of color center ensembles via universal qutrit control
authors: Antonio Verdú, Ana Teresa Gea-Caballero, Santiago Oviedo-Casado, Fedor Jelezko, Javier Prior
abstract: Dipolar interactions are a major source of decoherence in dense ensembles of color centers in diamond. Current protocols demand using bias magnetic fields detrimental in many scenarios. We present ZENITH (Zero-field Ensemble Neutralization via Interleaved Trilevel Handling), a pulsed sequence to cancel dipolar interactions among V-degenerate spin-1 systems. We reveal excellent coherence survival, compatibility with existing sensing sequences, and improved DC detection, advancing a general framework to control interacting degenerate multilevel systems, of broad interest in quantum technologies.
summary: The paper introduces the ZENITH protocol, a new pulse sequence designed to eliminate dipolar interactions in NV center ensembles at zero magnetic field. By using universal qutrit control, the method significantly extends coherence times and enables high-sensitivity DC magnetometry. This advancement is crucial for quantum technologies operating in environments where external magnetic fields cannot be applied.
main_problem: The decoherence of dense Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) center ensembles in diamond caused by dipolar interactions, especially in the absence of a bias magnetic field which is usually required to lift degeneracy.
main_result: The development of the ZENITH protocol, an 11-pulse sequence that achieves full dipolar decoupling at zero field, providing over two orders of magnitude improvement in coherence time and enabling enhanced DC magnetometry.
method: The authors use Average Hamiltonian Theory and a group-theoretical Cayley graph approach to design a pulse sequence that cancels both energy-conserving and non-energy-conserving dipolar terms via universal qutrit control.
model_or_system: Ensembles of Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, modeled as a spin-1 (qutrit) system with a V-degenerate ground state manifold subject to dipole-dipole interactions.
key_observables: Survival probability of qutrit coherent states, protection factor (coherence time ratio), and frequency estimation error for DC magnetometry.
important_parameters: Zero-field or low-bias magnetic field regime, dipolar interaction strength (J_T), and NV center concentrations (e.g., 15 ppm and 45 ppm).
main_assumptions: Rotating wave approximation (RWA), neglecting fast-rotating terms, and assuming the driving frequency is much smaller than the zero-field splitting (Omega << D).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and the ZENITH pulse sequence; Fig 2 illustrates the Cayley graph construction and decoupling logic; Fig 3 compares survival probability between FID and ZENITH; Fig S2 demonstrates DC signal sensing and error metrics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dipolar decoherence, defines the Hamiltonian and control framework, presents the ZENITH sequence construction using group theory, validates the protocol via numerical simulations, and demonstrates its utility for DC magnetometry.
why_interesting: It provides a general framework for controlling interacting degenerate multilevel systems (qutrits) and offers a method to suppress many-body interactions without external bias fields, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. ZENITH schematics (a) Experimental setup representation for arbitrary control of an ensemble of color centers. Two perpendicular microwave sources, orthogonal to the NV centers symmetry axis, enable universal control of the NV center ground state qutrit manifold. (b) Two-body dipolar interaction among electron spins, described here in relative spherical coordinates {r, θ, ϕ}. (c) A driving that matches the V-degenerate qutrit’s zero-field splitting ω ∼D enables double-state operators that create different superpositions of the states |1⟩and |−1⟩. (d) ZENITH sequence of π/2 rotations on different axes for dipolar coupling removal. Microwave rotations are implemented through pulses...

FIG. 2. Pictorial derivation of ZENITH dipolar decoupling sequence (a) Construction of the sequence that decouples the energy-conserving dipolar Hamiltonian HEC in Eq. (2). Minimal graph representing the connections between the different pulsed-transformed versions of HEC, denoted as gi ∈GX (balls) through the set of π/2 rotations V = {X, Y, ˜ X, ˜Y } (arrows), starting with a free-evolution time g1 = 1. All minimal solutions are achieved by two equally long paths (yellow and pink arrows), both starting from the same pulse (orange arrow). (b) Decoupling the non-energy conserving dipolar Hamiltonian HNC requires from Z pulses, obtained through the compositions ABA−1 and B−1AB, where the pair...

FIG. 3. ZENITH performance (a) Numerical time evolution of P, computed as the average of the survival probabilities of the states |↑⟩, |↓⟩, and |+⟩, on an ensemble of eight randomly distributed dipolar interacting qutrits under FID (solid blue line) and with the stroboscopic implementation of ZENITH for values of JT = 2, 1, 0.5, 0.1, respectively. For the faster decaying uncontrolled evolution and JT = 2 curves we average 10 cluster profiles to reach convergence, while for the rest a single cluster profile is enough, as no significant variations occur for the considered duration. (b) Protection factor as a function of JT. The factor for the protocol with JT = 0.1 is estimated to be Q ∼...

Figure 3(d) displays stroboscopic evolution on a cluster of eight NVs targeting a DC signal of strength µ = 0.1/T, both for FID and with ZENITH initiated on the optimal measurement state, considering two different experimentally relevant NV ensemble concentrations. While for FID dipolar coupling induces a fast decay of the signal, thereby preventing frequency estimation, the large coherence time availed by ZENITH yields clean oscillation from which the original DC signal can be reconstructed. Note that, as we show in Sec. VI of the SM [46], the optimal initial state can be achieved with a single pulse, and therefore does not introduce additional errors, nor it reduces the experiment time...
